posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked:
We’ve all probably had the experience of reading notes, either professional or in CT, that skew in the opposite direction of our impressions. It’s not unusual or strange, as palates and tastes are different. Some people like wines we don’t, and vice versa. Some people think wines are good that we think aren’t, and vice versa. But what about when the notes just don’t even sound like the same wine?

An obvious example here would be a flawed bottle, be it premox or brett or cork. Then it’s pretty easy to be sure your bottle is an abberation. Sometimes, though, it’s more puzzling.

I’ve thought about this a few times, most recently around a bottle of 2015 Sensuum Vertigo from Alexandre Filaine. (Yes, I know we don’t like 2015 champagne in general, let’s put that aside for the moment, if possible). The notes in CT mostly describe a wine in line with other vintages I’ve had of this wine “elegant, filagreed…profound,” “The finish is very complex with notes of chalky minerals,” “simply beautiful linear Champagne with great purity and balance.” Or, from @William_Kelley “a deep and textural Champagne that’s being disgorged with minimal dosage. Unwinding in the glass with aromas of ripe orchard fruit, freshly baked bread, white flowers and honeycomb, framed by some nutty top notes that hint of the complexity to come, it’s full-bodied, muscular and concentrated, with a layered, structured mid-palate and a long, resonant finish.”

These notes could describe many, if not all, the vintages of this wine I’ve had in the past. But the botle we opened was muddy, weird, with off-putting harsh citric acid notes and a dead finish. It wasn’t obviously flawed in any way, it just wasn’t in any way like either what we’ve come to expect, or like the notes.

So my question is this: what do people think when the bottle you had doesn’t match other notes at all? Like it was a completely different wine. Do you assume and off bottle? An awkward phase? Damage? Or do you figure it’s still simply a matter of palate difference?

posts.name: Dave Hendrik
posts.cooked:
Good question and one I have been thinking about lately. I recently opened a highly praised bottle of modestly priced Italian wine, and it was so different from the professional reviews that my first thought, which I don’t actually believe, was: I wonder if people are counterfeiting $35 bottles?
But I’m just chalking it up to bottle variation.

posts.name: Brian S t o t t e r
posts.cooked: We talked about this a bit during a wine dinner last night. Many variables at play. It certainly could be bottle variation or palate differences, so if looking at TNs from people on CT I seek out those whose palates are similar and I trust (yours included). In those cases where people describe a bottle as uninteresting, flat, etc. I question if they only judged it upon opening or whether they actually followed the wine over time to see if it evolved before writing their note. I’ve stopped reading too much into tasting notes written as part of much larger tastings for this reason, also because of the high likelihood that previously tasted wines may influence their impressions of what’s currently in the glass.
posts.display_username: Brian S t o t t e r

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
It’s certainly healthy to observe and acknowledge the wildly disparate TNs on CT. It reminds us that wine tasting is not an exact science, allowing us to proclaim exactly how a wine tastes and to insist it’s a ‘95 point wine’.

Sometimes I will suspect a wine featured in a TN was corked, when the note describes (for example) a lack of fruit, shrill acidity and harsh tannins. True, it could also be a problem of expectation, or of palate preferences, but my money generally on TCA when I see such outliers.

Wider than that, there are certainly differences in palates, but also in how people describe wine. We get a feel for both (but especially the former) when tasting in a sit-down group, sharing our thoughts on the wines we taste.

Variance in bottles? For sure, especially with cork-sealed bottles, but it’s telling that even screwcapped bottles can elicit widely varying TNs. Serving temperature / ambient conditions, state of mind, and whether / what food consumed with it are also genuinely valid variables.

My gut feeling is that human variance is the most significant factor, but there’s plenty more besides that.

posts.name: yaacovbarselah
posts.cooked: i’ve had many wines from my own cellar, from the same case, that are unrecognizable to me. So I have no idea what to think!

posts.name: Richard Flack
posts.cooked:
+1 on the contextual aspect. (At the risk of thread drift I recall a discussion at a tasting years ago about the impact of cleaning one’s teeth before going out to an event ) In similar vein when asked what is my favourite wine, I always ask what am I doing and with whom and what is the time of year, location etc.

Also on mild TCA. I’ve noticed a lot of casual drinkers don’t pay much attention to the nose and so TCA would manifest by apparent lack of fruit etc. Though whether that applies to many posting in CT I don’t know.

I’m not technical but Sarah’s bottle sounds like a fault of some kind to me. If she has more bottles, I really hope it’s not a ‘feature’.

posts.name: Tom DeBiase
posts.cooked:
Happens a lot. For me (and my wife) it often occurs
with the perception and “feel” of alcohol in a wine. No matter how good or complex the fruit, secondary notes or structure is, when the alcohol peeks through the wine becomes completely undrinkable to me.

About a year ago there were numerous notes on a very popular red wine. All of them noted the “beautiful fruit” “long finish” “ability to age” character of the wine. We tasted 3 different bottles over the course of a year, all were undrinkable to me. All because of the alcohol. Unusual example? Not at all, we’ve had this happen a lot over the years.

Tom

posts.name: Robert Dentice
posts.cooked: Lots of reasons but I find the concept of the biodynamic calendar and that certain days are better for tasting wine than others fascinating. I have a very high end stereo that on certain days sounds like the band is in my room and other days just does not sound that good? So on the days that a particular wine or all wines don’t taste great is it the wine or me?

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked: Totally get that. Some days it sounds better. Some days the bass is out of balance. But what about the days The Doors sounds like Bach?

posts.name: Tim Christenfeld
posts.cooked:
I would take this one step further: My own tasting notes sometimes disagree. When I am drinking a bottle from my cellar, I usually write the note without looking at previous notes for the same wine, and then am occasionally amused to find that I have just posted a note that disagrees with my earlier notes for the same wine. Most recently, I described a bottle of Egly-Ouriet Les Premices as having “biscuit flavor but not full-blown yeastiness,” while seven months earlier I had described it as having “unabashed yeastiness.”

Age matters. Context matters. Bottles vary. Humans are unreliable.

posts.name: yaacovbarselah
posts.cooked:
Sarah_Kirschbaum:

But what about the days The Doors sounds like Bach

Ray Manzarek famously influenced by JSB!

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked: I consider a lot of these examples as normal variation. When you’ve tasted a wine a number of times, you kind of establish a range of possibilities. These don’t confuse me - corks, temp, time open, age, palate differences, my own state on a given day. The ones that whack my brain are the ones I’d swear were not the wine being described. Maybe I’m the only one who gets hung up on this kind of outlier.

I wouldn’t be surprised if some people just want to “participate” and just copy paste notes from other people or similar wines assuming those notes will “make sense.” Silly, but kind’ve fraudulent TN’s.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked:
Sarah_Kirschbaum:

Do you assume and off bottle? An awkward phase? Damage? Or do you figure it’s still simply a matter of palate difference?

Possible to likely depending on age. Maybe. Possible to likely depending on age. Unlikely.

Off bottle (cork or bottling issues) or damage is my assumption most times depending on the age of the wine. Heat damage or other bottle abuse doesn’t show up immediately, so if it’s a young wine, I assume it’s probably a cork issue or bottling issue. (Even DIAM can cause cork issues, though they don’t taste like typical TCA wines.) If the wine is older, damage from heat and/or frequent transport somewhere over the years is my assumption.

As far as “muddy, weird, with off-putting harsh citric acid notes and a dead finish” that kind of checks all the boxes to me of a damaged wine of some sort, so I would just pour it down the drain and open another bottle and not think twice about it again.

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked: Hard to not think twice when it’s a relatively expensive hard to find wine you went to a lot of trouble to chase and have more in the cellar! I get what you’re saying, of course.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked: I suppose that’s where the “open another bottle” part comes in. If I didn’t/couldn’t open another one the same night, I’d queue it up for the next few weeks. If I get two bottles like that, I assume that it’s not for me and put it into the auction pile.

posts.name: Richard Flack
posts.cooked: Disambiguation required - Is that a good thing or a bad thing?

posts.name: Tony Gibson
posts.cooked:
Andrew_K:

that’s where the “open another bottle” part comes in

This. When a single bottle, especially the first time trying a particular wine, doesn’t line up at all with all previous notes, another bottle is needed.

To broaden this out a little, when repping wines it’s really something to see how much palate variation is out there. When you get to know a wine over many bottles and taste it with a many professionals, you see how inconsistent reactions are. Some bottles are either so good or so flawed that almost all the tasters will pick up on them. More often, people are all over the place and many are even inconsistent evaluating the same wine opened at different times. And these are all professional buyers I am talking about! It’s a really small percentage that nail it with high consistency. Hard to quantify exactly but I would say about 10-20% are good and consistent and close to 100% have a definitive opinion.

posts.name: Megan Joy
posts.cooked: To add to other comments on bottle variation: with Champagne and sparkling wines specifically there is also quite a bit more that happens on the individual bottle basis that could lead to one bottle being “off” from the majority. In still wine all the bottles (usually) shared one tank, and though there are things that can go wrong with closure, transportation and storage, the bulk of the wine creation is done. With Champagne the base wine would all come from one tank, but after that every bottle is its own mini-ferment (then individually disgorged and topped up) so you are looking at all of the potential issues that could arise during a hugely dynamic part of the wine’s development.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
There’s bottle variation and then there’s variant bottlings…

Many large production wines (and some small production wines, but that’s a subject for another day) are bottled in multiple runs from blends that may be subtly or more consequentially different.

Sometimes what you’re tasting actually is what you’re tasting.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Muddy as in turbid?

When was it disgorged?

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

Muddy as in turbid?

When was it disgorged?

Not turbid in appearance, but if you could translate that to taste, and a bit to texture, then yes - as if you’d stirred up the bottom of the river. Lacking in purity and delineation, blurred and texturally a little thick.

This was the 2023 disgorgement, 3 years on lees.

posts.name: Michael Kilfoyle
posts.cooked: Little thick - or a lot thick? Maybe a little lactic bacterial thing?

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked: No no, just not light on the toungue.

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked: I once blinded my experienced tasting group on 2 champagnes. What they didn’t know was that both were Salon 1996 from the same case. Wildly different opinions with nobody guessing the same wine. Bottle and palate variations . . .

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked: Yeah, that trick has been pulled many times. But I don’t put much stock in it because the strucure of the event makes people look for different wines, so they find different wines. It doesn’t surprise me at all when no one guesses there’s two bottles of the same wine.

posts.name: yaacovbarselah
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

Sometimes what you’re tasting actually is what you’re tasting.

the wine equivalent of “sometimes a cigar is just a cigar.”

posts.name: Saul Mutchnick
posts.cooked:
For the retail day-jobs I’ve had over the years, I’ve had a lot (too much) time looking up multiple critics’ reviews of the same wine, which really illustrates how variable tasting is—even for professionals.

I’m not talking about widespread disagreements on the quality of a wine (that’s to be expected) or it’s relative weight/acidity/etc (which certainly is influenced by what you drink regularly), but the basic organoleptic descriptions of a wine’s flavors can vary really broadly. One critic can talk about a wine being very red fruited and pure, then another critic will comment on its impressive plummy flavor that’s marked by oak flavors like vanilla.

I think we vary a lot as tasters (and the wines change some), though the fact that most of the time tasters across the board find a wine roughly similar really highlights how rare the extreme disparity–moments are.

posts.name: Tony Gibson
posts.cooked: I imported a wine that was already imported by another importer so the producer made a new label for us. This happens a lot! One of our reps presented it to a restaurant that had small panel of tasters. It was from a slightly obscure region and one of the staff said they had another bottle from the same region so they opened it to compare with ours. It was the same wine with a different label. Our rep said she had a hard time containing herself as the tasting panel argued over which wine was better and why.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
It does sound like your bottle was flawed in some way, and I’m not going to address the obvious general risk of bottle variation but the question about tasting notes is excellent.

I believe it was Andrew Jefford who wrote once that Robert Parker virtually invented the modern tasting note and he certainly wrote that Parker’s were the “gold standard”. I remember Michael Broadbent’s before, but they were never as detailed. Suddenly we had somebody who could describe what we would get if we bought the wine. This was a massive step forward, because unlike visual art forms, which anyone can judge on their own, according to their taste, wines, like books, need to be bought and opened. Literary criticism had been around for a very long time, wine criticism was just beginning.

Fast-forwarding to CT’s arrival twenty years ago, today anyone can write tasting notes (just like anyone can write book reviews on Amazon). In some ways, it’s very positive: there are many “amateurs” (in both senses) who write better than some professionals, some of whom are on WB: @Otto_Forsberg springs to mind - I’ve never, ever encountered the degree of forensic detail he goes to in his notes (and not even in his mother tongue). It’s probably only a matter of time before some pro review snaps him up, although they might insist on some editing!

But is he “right”? I would say that he’s fairly reliable, in that you can at least tell what he’s tasted, in view of the detail, but ultimately, taste is always going to be taste: it’s entirely subjective, however much some pros may try to pretend that they are “right”. This was Parker’s big mistake.

So of course we are going to disagree about what we drink and what it actually tastes like. One person’s red cherries will always be another’s plums. One thing that helps is to provide context, which many pros don’t: which other wines does it taste like? In which style is it made? That at least would eliminate a lot of misunderstandings.

Two other details that Otto includes, which are for me essential: the ABV level and the price. No matter whether the former is accurate and whether the latter is the same in one’s own country. They’re both better than nothing, which is what one gets from most pros.

But to return to the original question - “what about when the notes don’t even sound like the same wine?”

I think this is just inevitable. The whole concept of tasting notes is inherently flawed, even if there are ways they could be improved. Someone else’s notes are just someone else’s taste, on a specific time on a specific day, with a specific bottle. What did they eat with it? How were they feeling that day? Who knows what Parker would have written about Pavie 2003 had he eaten tripe for breakfast?!

posts.name: Kevin Porter
posts.cooked: Not helpful, as I know this isn’t an issue for you, but when I encounter such discrepancies I assume I have the palate of a yak.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: To me, sounds more like a bottle that just needed some more time to integrate its liqueur d’expedition. Fabrice disgorges to order, and the liqueur is added using an old machine in quite an “artisanal” manner.

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

To me, sounds more like a bottle that just needed some more time to integrate its liqueur d’expedition. Fabrice disgorges to order, and the liqueur is added using an old machine in quite an “artisanal” manner.

Thanks William. That’s interesting and helpful. That would make it more like a different wine altogether. I meant to grab an older disgorgement when I pulled it, but got it wrong. If that explanation is correct, would that suggest the other bottles of the same disgorgement could be just fine down the road after time on cork, or was the time needed before corking?
